examples.title: DDA Line Algorithm
examples.description: Digital Differential Analyzer algorithm for line drawing using floating-point arithmetic
examples.code:
ALGORITHM DDA_Line(x1, y1, x2, y2)
BEGIN
 dx ← x2 - x1
 dy ← y2 - y1
 
 steps ← MAX(ABS(dx), ABS(dy))
 
 x_increment ← dx / steps
 y_increment ← dy / steps
 
 x ← x1
 y ← y1
 
 FOR i ← 0 TO steps DO
 plot_pixel(ROUND(x), ROUND(y))
 x ← x + x_increment
 y ← y + y_increment
 END FOR
END
examples.explanation: DDA calculates the number of steps based on the larger of |dx| or |dy|. It then increments x and y by small fractional amounts at each step, rounding to nearest integer for pixel plotting.

examples.title: Bresenham's Line Algorithm
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Bresenham_Line(x1, y1, x2, y2)
BEGIN
 dx ← ABS(x2 - x1)
 dy ← ABS(y2 - y1)
 
 x ← x1
 y ← y1
 
 sx ← SIGN(x2 - x1)
 sy ← SIGN(y2 - y1)
 
 IF dx > dy THEN
 p ← 2*dy - dx
 FOR i ← 0 TO dx DO
 plot_pixel(x, y)
 IF p >= 0 THEN
 y ← y + sy
 p ← p + 2*dy - 2*dx
 ELSE
 p ← p + 2*dy
 END IF
 x ← x + sx
 END FOR
 ELSE
 p ← 2*dx - dy
 FOR i ← 0 TO dy DO
 plot_pixel(x, y)
 IF p >= 0 THEN
 x ← x + sx
 p ← p + 2*dx - 2*dy
 ELSE
 p ← p + 2*dx
 END IF
 y ← y + sy
 END FOR
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: Bresenham uses a decision parameter p that determines whether the next pixel should be at the same Y (or X for steep lines) or at Y+1. It uses only integer arithmetic - additions and multiplications by 2, which can be done with bit shifts.

examples.title: Wu's Anti-aliased Line Algorithm
examples.description: Line drawing with intensity based on proximity to ideal line
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Wu_Line(x1, y1, x2, y2)
BEGIN
 dx ← ABS(x2 - x1)
 dy ← ABS(y2 - y1)
 
 steps ← MAX(dx, dy)
 
 x_increment ← dx / steps
 y_increment ← dy / steps
 
 x ← x1
 y ← y1
 
 FOR i ← 0 TO steps DO
 intensity ← 1 - FRACTIONAL_PART(y)
 IF intensity > 0.5 THEN
 intensity ← 1 - intensity
 END IF
 
 plot_pixel(TRUNCATE(x), TRUNCATE(y), intensity)
 plot_pixel(TRUNCATE(x), TRUNCATE(y)+1, 1-intensity)
 
 x ← x + x_increment
 y ← y + y_increment
 END FOR
END
examples.explanation: Wu's algorithm assigns varying intensity to pixels based on their distance from the ideal line. Pixels closer to the line get higher intensity (brighter), creating a smooth anti-aliased appearance.
